knowledge_base.name: Table Tennis
knowledge_base.description: Technique, tactics, practice, and coaching concepts grounded in video demonstrations.

concepts.label: Backhand flick on the rise
concepts.short_definition: Close to the table, contact the backhand flick while the ball is rising to avoid being jammed.

evidence.excerpt: Close to the table, take it on the rise or at the peak.
evidence.reason: Frames the two available timing choices.

evidence.excerpt: At the peak, after two or three shots, I'm jammed completely.
evidence.reason: Shows the crowding problem created by waiting at close-table distance.

evidence.excerpt: When you're close to the table, your only real choice is to flick on the rise.
evidence.reason: States the corrective timing cue.

concepts.label: Creating space for the backhand
concepts.short_definition: Adjust body-to-ball distance so the racket has room for a natural backswing and forward arc.
concepts.detailed_definition: Spacing changes both the available stroke and contact timing. Too close produces a cramped push; roughly a racket or arm's length of working room permits acceleration and gives more timing options.

evidence.excerpt: The player stands too close to the ball.
evidence.reason: Introduces body-to-ball distance as the cause of the cramped stroke.

evidence.excerpt: There's no space between hand and ball, so all the player can do is push forward from the chest.
evidence.reason: Shows the mechanical consequence of insufficient working room.

evidence.excerpt: The peak of the bounce and your forward swing need roughly one racket length between them.
evidence.reason: Supplies a practical spacing reference for forward acceleration.

evidence.excerpt: Give yourself an arm's length of space. Now you can choose peak or rising.
evidence.reason: A second video connects distance from the table to available contact timing.

evidence.excerpt: Position the racket slightly left. This creates space for lateral force and a proper outside arc.
evidence.reason: Adds a Global TT Studio correction for creating backhand rotation room.

concepts.label: Backhand topspin body coordination
concepts.short_definition: A coordinated backhand topspin uses a square, open stance and a core-led coil-and-thrust so the forearm can accelerate without becoming disconnected from the body.
concepts.detailed_definition: Keep the feet nearly level, leave enough hip space for the backswing to remain inside the body line, and coil the abdomen inward with the preparation. Drive the core forward into contact while the forearm supplies the ordinary upward component. A static or permanently coiled body leaves the arm working alone and limits both power and consistency.

evidence.excerpt: Keep the feet nearly level, with the right foot at most slightly forward.
evidence.reason: Establishes a stable base for repeated backhand strokes.

evidence.excerpt: Open the hips and keep the backswing inside the body line.
evidence.reason: Prevents the contact from drifting too far outside the body.

evidence.excerpt: Coil the abdomen inward before driving forward into the active backhand.
evidence.reason: Identifies the core action that connects preparation to forward acceleration.

evidence.excerpt: A static body disconnects the backswing and prevents useful power generation.
evidence.reason: Contrasts coordinated core movement with an arm-only backhand.

evidence.excerpt: For ordinary strokes, the forearm supplies lift after contact instead of the shoulder.
evidence.reason: Separates the forearm's upward role from the upper arm's forward drive.

concepts.label: Under-ball contact for backspin serve
concepts.short_definition: Brush under the ball—rather than its back—to produce heavy backspin on the serve.

evidence.excerpt: Players contact the back of the ball, then wonder why it doesn't spin.
evidence.reason: Identifies the common contact-location error.

evidence.excerpt: To create spin, you must be under the ball. That's how it grips.
evidence.reason: Corrects the contact location.

evidence.excerpt: Bottom contact, maximum spin. Middle-lower, strong spin.
evidence.reason: Compares two useful under-ball contact regions.

concepts.label: Ball feel
concepts.short_definition: The combined ability to sense timing, space, contact, and tempo so the stroke adapts to each incoming ball.
concepts.detailed_definition: Ball feel is developed through variation rather than one fixed contact. Training different phases of the bounce and deliberately changing tempo builds a wider timing window and more adaptable touch.

evidence.excerpt: Ball feel includes touch, spatial awareness, and rhythm.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio explicitly defines the components of ball feel.

evidence.excerpt: The key is understanding rhythm. You must learn to control the tempo.
evidence.reason: Identifies rhythm control as a trainable component.

evidence.excerpt: Practice two timings—on the rise and at the start of the descent.
evidence.reason: Gives a concrete drill for expanding timing adaptability.

evidence.excerpt: Start on the rise, then switch to just after the peak.
evidence.reason: Demonstrates the timing variation in a short repeatable window.

concepts.label: Body-hand synchronization
concepts.short_definition: Coordinate body, arm, wrist, and fingers so power is transferred in sequence without the stroke disconnecting.
concepts.detailed_definition: Connection does not mean moving everything as one rigid block. A rally stroke should stop body and hand together, while a serve may deliberately sequence smaller links—fingers, wrist, forearm, then upper arm—to accelerate the racket.

evidence.excerpt: Body and hand must stop together. When the body stops, the hand stops.
evidence.reason: Gives the positive cue for a connected rally stroke.

evidence.excerpt: If the body stops but the hand still swings, that's disconnected.
evidence.reason: Provides a compact diagnostic for arm-body disconnection.

evidence.excerpt: Fingers move first, driving the wrist, then forearm, then upper arm.
evidence.reason: A second video shows coordinated sequencing for serve acceleration.

evidence.excerpt: Amateurs swing through as one block. Without decomposition, there's no spin.
evidence.reason: Contrasts useful sequencing with rigid, simultaneous movement.

evidence.excerpt: Think of your body in three segments—the upper body, core, and lower body.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio supplies a simple model for whole-body coordination.

evidence.excerpt: Movements are uncoordinated because these three segments are disconnected.
evidence.reason: Names disconnection between the body segments as the coordination failure.

concepts.label: Contact point
concepts.short_definition: The position and timing at which the racket meets the ball.
concepts.detailed_definition: For this forehand-loop example, contact is in front of the body near the top of the bounce; late contact reduces space and consistency.

evidence.excerpt: Practice different contact points—on the rise, at the peak, and on the fall.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio turns contact timing into a deliberate practice variable.

evidence.excerpt: The optimal contact point for heavy backspin is at the very beginning of its descent.
evidence.reason: Adds a spin-specific timing example from another Global TT Studio lesson.

evidence.excerpt: Make contact in the early descending phase.
evidence.reason: A concise repeatable summary of the same timing cue.

concepts.label: Forehand loop against backspin
concepts.short_definition: An opening forehand loop adjusted to lift and spin a backspin ball safely.
concepts.detailed_definition: Get the body and racket below the ball, choose contact height according to the available ball, and combine brushing with enough forward movement. A lower ball favors a safer high-arc spin loop; a higher ball permits a more forward power loop.

evidence.excerpt: Close the racket angle to feel brushing contact instead of direct impact.
evidence.reason: Explains why a closed face supports friction when opening against backspin.

evidence.excerpt: Against a falling backspin ball, begin with the racket lower than the ball.
evidence.reason: Gives a concrete vertical-position cue for clearing backspin.

evidence.excerpt: Draw the racket back, swing outward, then return inward in a small semicircle.
evidence.reason: Defines the outward arc and connects a larger arc with heavier incoming spin.

evidence.excerpt: Brush forward and upward while driving into the ball, rather than lifting only vertically.
evidence.reason: Distinguishes a usable opening-loop direction from a purely upward action.

evidence.excerpt: Drop your shoulder, lower your legs, and drop your wrist.
evidence.reason: Summarizes preparation for a heavy-backspin opening loop.

visual_source.note: Coach demonstrates the lowered stance and shoulder-racket preparation.

evidence.excerpt: Keep contact roughly level with the table for more brush; raise it somewhat for a forward power loop.
evidence.reason: Connects the ball height to the safer or more forceful loop choice.

visual_source.note: Live-ball demonstrations compare contact heights for brushing and power looping.

evidence.excerpt: Combine racket rotation with forward movement through contact; rotating in place will not clear the net.
evidence.reason: Prevents a brush-only action that lacks forward travel.

visual_source.note: Close live demonstration contrasts forward movement with wrist rotation in place.

concepts.label: Forehand loop common errors
concepts.short_definition: Common loop failures include pushing before brushing, lifting only upward, rotating the wrist in place, reaching, and using maximum power before the stroke is stable.
concepts.detailed_definition: These errors break the intended sequence or contact geometry. Useful corrections preserve a curved racket path, cover the ball, move forward through contact, maintain spacing, and develop the stroke below maximum effort before adding speed.

evidence.excerpt: If the arm pushes first, the ball leaves the racket before the later brushing action can affect it.
evidence.reason: Explains why a push-then-wrist sequence does not create useful spin.

visual_source.note: Ball-on-racket demonstration contrasts pushing first with wrapping and brushing.

evidence.excerpt: Brush over the top of the ball; the path should not simply travel upward.
evidence.reason: Corrects loops that fly long because the action is too vertical.

visual_source.note: Live loops demonstrate covering the ball from different table distances.

evidence.excerpt: Racket rotation must combine with forward movement; rotating the wrist in place will not send the ball over.
evidence.reason: Corrects an isolated wrist action without forward travel.

visual_source.note: Close demonstration contrasts forward action with rotation in place.

concepts.label: Forehand loop power generation
concepts.short_definition: Generate forehand-loop power with a relaxed body-led backswing, forward drive through contact, late acceleration, and a compact braking action.
concepts.detailed_definition: Power is transferred through a coordinated sequence rather than by pulling the arm back or tensing early. The torso carries a relaxed arm into position; the racket drives into the ball, accelerates late, and the forearm's braking action helps turn linear speed into a fast, compact finish.

evidence.excerpt: Use body rotation to bring the arm back rather than deliberately pulling the arm backward.
evidence.reason: Establishes a relaxed body-led preparation.

visual_source.note: Shadow-stroke comparison shows body rotation carrying the arm.

evidence.excerpt: Drop down, drive forward into the ball, and then brake the arm quickly.
evidence.reason: Defines the drive-snap sequence used to transfer power through contact.

visual_source.note: Repeated shadow strokes show the forward drive and compact snap.

evidence.excerpt: Acceleration must be combined with forceful braking.
evidence.reason: Explains why acceleration without a controlled finish is insufficient.

visual_source.note: Live-ball and shadow comparisons show loose versus forceful finishing.

concepts.label: Forehand loop practice progression
concepts.short_definition: Build the loop from coordinated shadow movement to stable fixed-point strokes at moderate effort before adding speed, variation, and match pressure.
concepts.detailed_definition: Early practice should reduce difficulty so the player can stabilize timing, contact, and body-arm structure. Shadow practice develops coordination without ball pressure; fixed-point feeding at roughly 30–50 percent effort makes the movement repeatable. After that base is stable, practice different contact timings and introduce variations in pace, height, depth, and placement before adding match pressure.

evidence.excerpt: Do not use full power; use about 40 to 50 percent strength.
evidence.reason: Establishes a controllable intensity for fixed-point learning.

visual_source.note: Fixed-point feeding demonstrates stable moderate-power loops.

evidence.excerpt: Use 30–40 percent power to establish a solid fundamental swing.
evidence.reason: A second coach independently recommends reduced effort for technique stability.

visual_source.note: Controlled live loops demonstrate the recommended practice intensity.

evidence.excerpt: Hold the racket and do shadow practice without hitting the ball to develop coordination.
evidence.reason: Places unloaded coordination work before ball drills.

visual_source.note: Segmented shadow swings demonstrate the coordination exercise.

evidence.excerpt: Alternate between contacting the ball on the rise and just after the peak.
evidence.reason: Progresses a stable loop into deliberate timing adjustment.

evidence.excerpt: Ask for slower, higher, faster, and awkwardly placed balls instead of perfect feeds.
evidence.reason: Adds controlled variability so timing adapts to changing ball quality.

concepts.label: Forehand loop spacing and contact zone
concepts.short_definition: Preserve enough room from the table and body for the ball to enter a repeatable contact zone without reaching or getting jammed.
concepts.detailed_definition: Good spacing is dynamic rather than a single fixed distance. Start far enough from the table to complete the stroke, adjust with the feet to the ball's depth, and let the ball approach the lead-leg area so body rotation can transfer power without overreaching.

evidence.excerpt: Leave at least one upper-arm's distance when playing a forehand topspin loop.
evidence.reason: Supplies a practical starting reference for table distance.

visual_source.note: Coach demonstrates the space required between player and table.

evidence.excerpt: Let the ball come close, near the front of the leg, before striking.
evidence.reason: Locates the body-relative contact zone.

visual_source.note: Live feeds contrast a close contact zone with reaching in front.

evidence.excerpt: The distance between your hand and the ball is crucial.
evidence.reason: Shows that landing depth must be read before choosing the preparation size.

visual_source.note: Ball placement examples show how landing depth changes hand-ball distance.

concepts.label: Forehand loop swing path and racket angle
concepts.short_definition: The racket travels on an outer arc while a suitably closed face brushes and drives through the ball.
concepts.detailed_definition: A loop is not merely a larger drive. The racket head follows an outer arc, covers the ball with a closed face, and moves forward through contact. The precise balance of impact and brush changes with incoming spin, height, and the desired arc.

evidence.excerpt: Brushing over the ball with a closed racket generates spin.
evidence.reason: Demonstrates covering the ball rather than brushing vertically.

visual_source.note: Live-ball forehand loops show the covered racket face.

evidence.excerpt: Looping requires an outer-arc racket trajectory.
evidence.reason: Distinguishes the loop path from a drive.

visual_source.note: Front-view racket overlays compare the drive path with the outer loop arc.

evidence.excerpt: When looping, focus more on the feeling of brushing the ball.
evidence.reason: Provides a beginner-friendly way to develop loop contact.

visual_source.note: Fixed-point forehand practice demonstrates brushing contact at reduced power.

concepts.label: Forehand loop timing and rhythm
concepts.short_definition: Time the forehand loop by waiting for the ball, staying loose through preparation, and accelerating at contact.
concepts.detailed_definition: Incoming pace changes the tempo, but the player still avoids rushing the backswing. The body and racket prepare with the ball, remain relaxed, and release speed late at the chosen contact point.

evidence.excerpt: Your swing should be relaxed until just before the contact point, not forceful from the beginning.
evidence.reason: Defines late-stage acceleration.

visual_source.note: Coach demonstrates the relaxed approach and late racket acceleration.

evidence.excerpt: For a slow ball the rhythm is 'wait, loop'; for a fast ball it is 'wait, quick.'
evidence.reason: Demonstrates pace-dependent rhythm.

visual_source.note: Consecutive fed balls demonstrate waiting and changing the swing tempo.

evidence.excerpt: Stay relaxed before the ball; accelerate and apply force only at contact.
evidence.reason: Corrects premature tension and rushed acceleration.

visual_source.note: Live-ball strokes illustrate tension before contact and the late release.

concepts.label: Forehand loop
concepts.short_definition: A forehand topspin stroke that brushes the ball with forward-and-upward acceleration.
concepts.detailed_definition: The racket brushes the upper back of the ball while accelerating forward and upward, with contact in front of the body.

evidence.excerpt: For a loop, the backswing is much larger, creating a bigger swing radius.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio contrasts loop mechanics with a compact drive.

evidence.excerpt: To generate topspin and a proper arc, use a swing path that brushes up on the ball.
evidence.reason: Gives the purpose of the loop swing path.

evidence.excerpt: The direction is forward and upward. Drive forward into the ball as you brush.
evidence.reason: Adds a heavy-backspin loop example from another video.

evidence.excerpt: Your swing should be relaxed until just before the contact point, not forceful from the beginning.
evidence.reason: Explains late acceleration for a powerful, controllable loop.

visual_source.note: Coach demonstrates the relaxed approach and late racket acceleration.

evidence.excerpt: Drop your shoulder, lower your legs, and drop your wrist.
evidence.reason: Summarizes the three-downs preparation for looping heavy backspin.

visual_source.note: Coach demonstrates the lowered stance and shoulder-racket preparation.

evidence.excerpt: Brushing over the ball with a closed racket generates spin.
evidence.reason: Demonstrates covering the ball instead of brushing only upward.

visual_source.note: Live-ball forehand loops show the covered racket face.

evidence.excerpt: For a slow ball the rhythm is 'wait, loop'; for a fast ball it is 'wait, quick.'
evidence.reason: Shows how timing changes with incoming pace while retaining a wait phase.

visual_source.note: Consecutive fed balls demonstrate waiting and changing the swing tempo.

evidence.excerpt: The ideal contact point is as the ball is about to reach your leg, where you can transfer power.
evidence.reason: Locates the modern loop contact point relative to the body.

visual_source.note: Live feeds demonstrate letting the ball enter the contact zone near the lead leg.

evidence.excerpt: When looping, focus more on the feeling of brushing the ball.
evidence.reason: Gives beginners a simple contact-feel priority.

visual_source.note: Fixed-point forehand practice demonstrates brushing contact at reduced power.

evidence.excerpt: Looping requires an outer-arc racket trajectory.
evidence.reason: Distinguishes the loop path from a larger drive.

visual_source.note: Front-view racket overlays compare the drive path with the outer loop arc.

evidence.excerpt: Stay relaxed before the ball; accelerate and apply force only at contact.
evidence.reason: Corrects premature tension in the forward swing.

visual_source.note: Live-ball strokes illustrate tension before contact and the late release.

evidence.excerpt: A high-arc loop is essentially a power loop with extra safety added.
evidence.reason: Introduces the tradeoff between spin, power, arc, and consistency.

visual_source.note: Alternating live-ball examples compare the power loop and safer high-arc loop.

evidence.excerpt: Keep contact roughly level with the table for more brush; raise it somewhat for a forward power loop.
evidence.reason: Connects contact height to loop selection.

visual_source.note: Live-ball demonstrations compare contact heights for brushing and power looping.

evidence.excerpt: Combine racket rotation with forward movement through contact; rotating in place will not clear the net.
evidence.reason: Corrects an isolated wrist-only loop action.

visual_source.note: Close live demonstration contrasts forward movement with wrist rotation in place.

concepts.label: Ground connection and loaded legs
concepts.short_definition: Keep the legs engaged and connect to the floor so force can travel from the ground into the stroke.
concepts.detailed_definition: Ground connection is not limited to rally strokes. The same principle appears in serving, where weight transfer and a stomp can multiply the force delivered to the racket.

evidence.excerpt: Your legs must carry tension—engaged like loaded springs.
evidence.reason: Defines the intended leg engagement without locking the knees.

evidence.excerpt: When your legs are loaded, your upper body can actually generate power.
evidence.reason: Connects the base of support to upper-body power production.

evidence.excerpt: The stomp creates a body-ground connection. It's a force multiplier.
evidence.reason: Demonstrates the same ground-connection principle in a serve from another video.

evidence.excerpt: The issue is your legs are not engaged; they're like wet noodles.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio diagnoses the opposite of loaded legs.

evidence.excerpt: Engage your legs. Think of your legs as springs that store energy to release.
evidence.reason: Provides the positive spring analogy from the new channel.

concepts.label: Loose-back, tight-release rhythm
concepts.short_definition: Keep the backhand backswing loose, then tighten explosively through the forward release.

evidence.excerpt: My forearm seems set, but underneath it's extremely relaxed.
evidence.reason: Isolates the relaxed preparation phase.

evidence.excerpt: Relax on the way back, tighten on the way out.
evidence.reason: Compresses the full tension rhythm into one repeatable cue.

evidence.excerpt: Use about 30% grip pressure on the backswing, then tighten toward 80% at contact.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio quantifies the relaxed-to-tight rhythm.

evidence.excerpt: Grip firmly at impact to prevent racket wobble and errors.
evidence.reason: Explains why tension rises at the release phase.

concepts.label: Generating spin at contact
concepts.short_definition: Create spin by allowing the racket head to accelerate and brush the correct part of the ball instead of squeezing or pushing rigidly.
concepts.detailed_definition: Spin emerges from both freedom and precision: a relaxed grip permits last-moment racket-head speed, rotation adds brushing force, and the contact region determines the resulting spin.

evidence.excerpt: The space between thumb and index finger is practically open. Then I whip through.
evidence.reason: Shows how localized grip relaxation permits racket acceleration.

evidence.excerpt: The tighter I hold, the less friction I can generate. The looser I hold, the more friction I create.
evidence.reason: States the grip-tension relationship directly.

evidence.excerpt: I add a slight rotational twist to my left side in the backhand. This adds spin and power.
evidence.reason: A second video illustrates rotation as another source of brushing speed.

evidence.excerpt: The faster the racket head rotates, the spinnier the ball.
evidence.reason: A third video isolates racket-head rotational speed as the spin generator.

evidence.excerpt: To create spin, you must be under the ball. Bottom contact, maximum spin.
evidence.reason: Shows that racket speed must be paired with the correct contact region.

evidence.excerpt: First, use your fingers to create friction. After friction is applied, tighten the grip and follow through.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio separates brushing friction from the later grip-and-follow-through phase.

evidence.excerpt: Use your fingers to create friction, then tighten your grip to 80%.
evidence.reason: Repeats the sequence specifically for looping.

concepts.label: Serve action decomposition
concepts.short_definition: Sequence fingers, wrist, forearm, and upper arm instead of swinging the serve as one block.

evidence.excerpt: Spin comes from decomposition—separating your power sources.
evidence.reason: Introduces the principle.

evidence.excerpt: Fingers move first, driving the wrist, then forearm, then upper arm.
evidence.reason: Gives the explicit acceleration sequence.

evidence.excerpt: Control your forearm and upper arm, then use your fingers to accelerate.
evidence.reason: Ends with a practical correction cue.

concepts.label: Short-serve third-ball attack
concepts.short_definition: Serve short to invite a slow push, then attack the first available return.

evidence.excerpt: The first ball is easiest to attack, especially after a short serve and push return.
evidence.reason: Introduces the third-ball opportunity.

evidence.excerpt: Serve short and light spin; the push comes back slow, perfect to loop or power drive.
evidence.reason: States the serve, expected return, and intended attack.

evidence.excerpt: The best serve is one that sets up your strongest attack.
evidence.reason: Global TT Studio reframes serve quality around the follow-up rather than the ace.

evidence.excerpt: What they really fear is your follow-up attack.
evidence.reason: Explains the tactical pressure created by a planned next ball.

evidence.excerpt: After your serve, look for a weak return to set up a powerful third-ball attack.
evidence.reason: A second Global TT Studio video states the complete pattern in one clip.

concepts.label: Spin loop versus power loop
concepts.short_definition: Choose the balance of brush, impact, arc, and forward speed according to the incoming ball and acceptable risk.
concepts.detailed_definition: A high-arc spin loop adds clearance and consistency; a power loop uses a more favorable contact height and greater forward impact. These are points on a continuum: brushing manages arc and incoming spin, while forward drive manages depth and speed.

evidence.excerpt: A high-arc loop is essentially a power loop with extra safety added.
evidence.reason: Frames spin and power loops as a safety-quality tradeoff.

visual_source.note: Alternating live-ball examples compare the power and high-arc loops.

evidence.excerpt: Keep contact roughly level with the table for more brush; raise it for a forward power loop.
evidence.reason: Connects loop choice to the available contact height.

visual_source.note: Live feeds compare contact heights and resulting loop trajectories.

evidence.excerpt: Brushing controls the height of the arc, while driving forward controls its depth.
evidence.reason: Gives a usable rule for blending spin and forward impact.

visual_source.note: Racket-path demonstration separates brushing from forward drive.

How to Find the Right Contact Point When Looping Underspin💥🏓

navigation.title: Table tennis knowledge map
navigation.introduction: Browse strokes, movement principles, training methods, and tactical patterns as a connected learning tree. Start with a shot family, then move into its motion parts, corrections, drills, and match use.

sections.label: Shots and strokes
sections.description: The main actions used to serve, receive, attack, and rally.

children.label: Push
children.description: Backspin control strokes used to keep the ball low, receive serves, and prevent an easy attack.

children.label: Forehand push
children.description: Forehand-side push mechanics, placement, spin variation, and recovery.

children.label: Backhand push
children.description: Backhand-side push mechanics, touch, placement, and receive use.

children.label: Counter
children.description: Compact counterhit and counterdrive strokes played against pace or topspin.

children.label: Forehand counter
children.description: Forehand counter mechanics, timing, placement, and transition into stronger attacks.

children.label: Backhand counter
children.description: Backhand counter mechanics, timing, compactness, and rally use.

children.label: Loop
children.description: Topspin attacks that combine brushing, forward force, arc, and recovery.

children.label: Forehand loop
children.description: Forehand loop fundamentals, variations, motion parts, corrections, and practice.

children.label: Variations and ball selection
children.description: Adjust the forehand loop to backspin, contact height, arc, power, and risk.

children.label: Motion parts
children.description: Preparation, spacing, contact, racket path, timing, acceleration, and finish.

children.label: Preparation and spacing
children.description: Create room, load the body, and place the ball in a usable contact zone.

children.label: Contact and racket path
children.description: Contact location, racket angle, brushing direction, and forward movement.

children.label: Timing, power, and release
children.description: Coordinate the body and arm, wait for the ball, accelerate late, and finish compactly.

children.label: Learning and correction
children.description: Progressions, controlled drills, and diagnosis of common failures.

children.label: Backhand loop
children.description: Backhand loop fundamentals, variations, motion parts, corrections, and practice.

children.label: Block
children.description: Short defensive and countering strokes that redirect an opponent's topspin.

children.label: Forehand block
children.description: Forehand block angle, timing, absorption, placement, and active variations.

children.label: Backhand block
children.description: Backhand block angle, compact structure, timing, and active variations.

children.label: Flick
children.description: Over-the-table attacks against short or half-long balls.

children.label: Forehand flick
children.description: Forehand over-table attack mechanics and receive choices.

children.label: Backhand flick
children.description: Backhand over-table attack timing, spacing, and spin contact.

children.label: Serve
children.description: Starting strokes organized by spin, placement, and intended third-ball pattern.

children.label: Serve and third-ball patterns
children.description: Use serve placement and spin to shape a predictable return for the next attack.

sections.label: Movement and contact fundamentals
sections.description: Principles shared across several strokes rather than belonging to one shot only.

children.label: Body organization
children.description: Ground connection, usable spacing, and coordination through the kinetic chain.

children.label: Racket-ball interaction
children.description: Reading the ball and controlling where, when, and how the racket contacts it.

sections.label: Training and drills
sections.description: Ways to acquire, stabilize, diagnose, and pressure-test technique.

children.label: Technique acquisition
children.description: Shadow work, fixed-point feeding, moderate-power repetition, and progressive complexity.

children.label: Error correction
children.description: Contrast incorrect and correct movement patterns with short repeatable examples.

sections.label: Strategy and point patterns
sections.description: Selecting shots and arranging earlier balls to create a favorable attack.

children.label: Serve and third-ball attack
children.description: Use serve placement and spin to shape a predictable return for the next attack.

children.label: Attack selection
children.description: Match loop height, spin, pace, and risk to the incoming ball and tactical situation.
